Node.js Basics, Part 2: Writing JavaScript Right in the REPL
This one's pretty short. It's mostly a note about how you can use Node's REPL mode to write JavaScript straight in the terminal, without creating a file first.
Node.js Basics, Part 1: Install It, Meet the V8 Engine, and Run Hello World

Kicking off another basics series — this one's a set of notes I'm writing to get my own head straight on Node.js concepts and development. In this first post we'll cover what Node.js actually is, the V8 engine behind it, why it's worth using, how to install it, and how to run your very first Hello World.
End of the series — kind of. It's a wrap-up, but also a beginning, since there's still plenty I haven't covered. Maybe I'll write some bonus chapters later.
Bonus chapter — adding ejs. If you've used Node.js Express, you've probably used ejs. Like the pug bonus chapter, this won't be pushed to the GitHub repo since not everyone uses these templates.
Bonus chapter — for people who use pug/jade (myself included). pug is a template language that simplifies HTML. Knowing more templating languages is good for a frontend dev — you type less.

We've covered most of the common Gulp packages and use cases. One more — GitHub Pages. If you've built a portfolio page and want to host it somewhere, GitHub Pages is a solid option. This post covers deploying to it.
When frontend work wraps up and we hand off `public` to the backend, it needs to be the latest version. So we need a command to wipe `public` and regenerate it.
We've got browser-sync for local dev — but do we have to restart it every time we change a file? Nope. Gulp has a `watch` API that monitors file changes and re-runs the right tasks automatically.
Time for browser-sync — a local dev essential for spinning up an instant local server.
Gulp Series: Running Tasks in Sequence/Parallel
As tasks pile up, you'll want a single command to run them all instead of typing gulp scss, gulp babel one by one.
